frameworks.name: hierarchical timescale organization
frameworks.one_line: intrinsic timescales of neural dynamics follow anatomical hierarchy across the brain

frameworks.name: temporal receptive windows
frameworks.one_line: regions integrate information over windows that lengthen with hierarchical depth

frameworks.name: resting-state functional connectivity
frameworks.one_line: intrinsic timescale gradients predict individual functional connectivity features

core_question: Do intrinsic timescales of spontaneous brain activity follow hierarchical spatial gradients across cortex and subcortical structures?
key_assumptions: temporal autocorrelation of resting activity indexes intrinsic timescale
anatomical hierarchy organizes both task-evoked and spontaneous dynamics
whole-brain fMRI captures dynamics across cortex and subcortex
manipulations: mapping timescales across cortical regions at rest
comparing cortical timescale gradients to striatum, thalamus, and cerebellum
examining timescale variation along the hippocampal long axis
key_findings: resting-state timescales follow hierarchical spatial gradients across human cortex
intrinsic timescale gradients produce frequency differences among large-scale networks
timescales predict individual-specific functional connectivity features
cortical gradients are topographically mirrored in striatum, thalamus, cerebellum, and hippocampus
interpretations: hierarchical timescales are a global organizing principle of mammalian brain function
subcortical dynamics inherit a topographic timescale map from cortex
